    Home › Manufacturers › Douglas & McDonnell Douglas

    Douglas & McDonnell Douglas

    United States · Founded 1921

    From the DC-3 to the last great trijets — the manufacturer Boeing absorbed.

    Aircraft here3
    Longest61.62 m
    Combined built1,202
    First flights1958–1990
    The company

    The story so far

    Douglas Aircraft once ruled the skies: the DC-3 made air travel a business, and the DC-8 took it into the jet age, becoming the only airliner in history to exceed the speed of sound in a dive. Merged with McDonnell in 1967, the combined company built the DC-10 and the MD-11 — the last three-engine widebodies ever produced.

    The MD-11's commercial failure, alongside a shrinking military portfolio, ended the company's independence: Boeing acquired McDonnell Douglas in 1997. Its aircraft fly on mostly as freighters, and its trijets remain among the most distinctive silhouettes ever built.

    The line-up

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
